bibliographic_data.title: SECOMP: Formally Secure Compilation of Compartmentalized C Programs
bibliographic_data.abstract: Undefined behavior in C often causes devastating security vulnerabilities. One practical mitigation is compartmentalization, which allows developers to structure large programs into mutually distrustful compartments with clearly specified privileges and interactions. In this paper we introduce SECOMP, a compiler for compartmentalized C code that comes with machine-checked proofs guaranteeing that the scope of undefined behavior is restricted to the compartments that encounter it and become dynamically compromised. These guarantees are formalized as the preservation of safety properties against adversarial contexts, a secure compilation criterion similar to full abstraction, and this is the first time such a strong criterion is proven for a mainstream programming language.

core_content.novelty_claim: SECOMP introduces formally secure compilation of compartmentalized C programs, providing machine-checked proofs that restrict the scope of undefined behavior to dynamically compromised compartments and formalize this as a secure compilation criterion.
core_content.key_methodology: SECOMP extends the CompCert compiler with isolated compartments that can only interact via procedure calls and returns. It adapts all passes and optimizations of CompCert to handle these compartments, ensuring formally secure compilation for C programs.
outcomes: Formal guarantees against undefined behavior in compartmentalized C code
First secure compilation criterion proven for a mainstream programming language
core_content.full_text_markdown: SECOMP is designed to address the pervasive issue of undefined behavior in C, which often leads to severe security vulnerabilities. The paper introduces SECOMP as a compiler that can handle compartmentalized C programs with formally verified proofs ensuring that any undefined behavior encountered by a compartment remains confined within it and does not compromise other compartments. This is achieved through an extension of the CompCert compiler, incorporating isolated compartments that interact only via procedure calls and returns. By adapting the passes and optimizations of CompCert to this new setting, SECOMP provides a secure compilation framework for C programs. The approach involves proving robust safety preservation (RSP) as a secure compilation criterion, which ensures that any low-level attack against a compiled program's safety properties can be traced back to arbitrary compartments with the same interface and privileges.
